results.possibleIdentity: Robert K. Golden was a technical specialist and engineer active in the fields of computer systems and communications during the mid-20th century. In the early 1950s (approximately 1950–1952), he served as a Junior Researcher at the Institute for Numerical Analysis (INA) at UCLA, a significant early hub for computer development and numerical mathematics under the National Bureau of Standards. Following his research at INA, Golden became an engineer for IBM Corporation, where he was based in Hyde Park and Peekskill, New York. During his tenure at IBM in the late 1950s and early 1960s, he co-developed early data processing and information retrieval technologies, resulting in several patents, including an 'Information Reproducing System' (US Patent 3,048,333) and a 'Data Classifying Device' (US Patent 3,052,407). By the late 1960s and early 1970s, Golden worked as a self-employed consultant in New York City, specializing in computer systems and communications. In this capacity, he was associated with the non-profit organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which facilitated collaborations between artists and technical professionals. His background in tele-processing and automatic control systems at IBM made him a valuable technical resource for the E.A.T. network during the era of early computer art and telecommunications experiments.
